subject: Physics
options: inversely proportional to r in the region r<a and uniform throughout in the region  r>a

solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field inside the conducting wire is given by

Bin =μ0Jr2   ...(1)

The formula to calculate the magnetic field outside the conducting wire is given by

Bout =μ0Ja22r   ...(2)

From equation (1), it can be concluded that the magnetic field is directly proportional to the radial distance inside the wire, whereas from equation (2), it can be concluded that the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the radial distance outside the wire.

solution:
The force experienced by the charge particle is perpendicular to the instantaneous velocity at all instants as F→=qv→×B→.

Hence, the magnetic force cannot bring any change in the speed of the charged particle. Since, the speed remains constant, the kinetic energy also remains constant.

& =0.25 \times 0.2 \\
& =50 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N} \\
& =50 \mathrm{mN}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
For a long cylindrical conductor with uniform current distribution, apply Ampere's law to find the magnetic field.
At distance $r$ from the axis (inside the conductor, $r < R$): $B(r) = \frac{\mu_0 I r}{2\pi R^2}$ (proportional to $r$)
At the axis ($r = 0$): $B = 0$ (minimum)
At the surface ($r = R$): $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi R}$ (maximum)
Analyzing the statements:
A. Maximum at ends and minimum at midpoint - FALSE. This describes behavior along the axis, but the magnetic field depends on radial distance, not axial position.
B. Maximum at the axis of the conductor - FALSE. The field is zero at the axis.
C. Minimum at the surface of the conductor - FALSE. The field is maximum at the surface.
D. Minimum at the axis of the conductor - TRUE. The field is zero at $r = 0$, making it the minimum.
E. Same at all points in the cross-section - FALSE. The field varies from 0 at the axis to maximum at the surface.
Only D is correct.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the distance between the wires be $d = 8\text{ cm}$. The distance of the midpoint from each wire is $r = \dfrac{d}{2} = 4\text{ cm} = 0.04\text{ m}$.

The current in each wire is $I = 30\text{ A}$.

Since the currents flow in opposite directions, the magnetic fields produced by both wires at the midpoint point in the same direction and add up.

The total magnetic field at the midpoint is:

$B_{\text{net}} = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi r}$

Substituting the given values:

$B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 30}{\pi \times 0.04}$

$B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04}$

$B_{\text{net}} = 3000 \times 10^{-7}\text{ T}$

$B_{\text{net}} = 300 \times 10^{-6}\text{ T} = 300\ \mu\text{T}$

Answer: $300$

solution: Net magnetic field due to loop $A B C D$ at $O$ is $$ \begin{aligned} & B=B_{A B}+B_{B C}+B_{C D}+B_{D A} \\ & =0+\frac{\mu_o I}{4 \pi a} \times \frac{\pi}{6}+0-\frac{\mu_o I}{4 \pi b} \times \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\mu_o I}{24 a}-\frac{\mu_o I}{24 b}=\frac{\mu_o I}{24 a b}(b-a) \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic field in between because of each will be in opposite direction
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text {in between }} & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi x} \hat{j}-\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi(2 d-x)}(-\hat{j}) \\
& =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 d-x}\right](\hat{j})
\end{aligned}
$$
at $\quad x=d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=0$
for $\quad x < d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=(\hat{j})$
for $\quad x>d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=(-\hat{j})$
towards $\mathrm{x}$ net magnetic field will add up and direction will be $(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$
towards $\mathrm{x}^{\prime}$ net magnetic field will add up and direction will be $(\hat{\mathrm{j}})$

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$

Substituting the given values, $B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{2 \times 0.1} = 4\pi \times 10^{-6}$ T

The magnetic energy density is $u = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$

$u = \dfrac{(4\pi \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{16\pi^2 \times 10^{-12}}{8\pi \times 10^{-7}} = 2\pi \times 10^{-5}$ J/m$^3$

The volume of the small cube is $V = (1 \text{ mm})^3 = (10^{-3} \text{ m})^3 = 10^{-9}$ m$^3$

The magnetic energy stored inside the cube is $U = u \times V$

$U = 2\pi \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-9} = 2\pi \times 10^{-14}$ J

Using $\pi = 3.14$, we get $U = 2 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-14} = 6.28 \times 10^{-14}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-14}$ J, we get $\alpha = 6.28$

Answer: $6.28$
